coredata.dc:title: Red-emissive carbon dots based fluorescent and smartphone-integrated paper sensors for sensitive detection of carbendazim
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Haipeng
Yang, Ruiqi
Lei, Doudou
Ma, Xiao
Zhang, Dingkun
Li, Peng
Wang, Jiabo
Zhou, Lidong
Kong, Weijun
coredata.dc:description: Trace detection of carbendazim (CBZ), a worldwide used pesticide, in crops and foods is important and urgent to reduce risks to human health. Herein, newly red-emissive carbon dots (R-CDs) based fluorescent and smartphone-integrated paper sensors were developed for highly-selective, and -sensitive rapid sensing of CBZ. R-CDs were synthesized from citric acid and urea via a DMF solvothermal method, which showed excellent analytical performance due to abundant carboxyl groups on the surface and long emission wavelength to avoid autofluorescence interference of matrix. According to the red fluorescence quenching by CBZ via π-π interaction and photoinduced electron-transfer effect, the newly fluorescent nano sensor could realize CBZ sensing within 3 min with high selectivity out of the other six interfering pesticides, as well as fascinating sensitivity with an ultralow detection limit of 3.5 ng/mL. The practicable application in the spiked medicinal Lotus seeds samples exhibited satisfactory recoveries of 95.5%-108.3%. The portable paper-based sensor fabricated by depositing R-CDs on the filter paper enabled quantitative readout of test strip results assisted with a smartphone, providing a rapid on-site tool for CBZ detection in medicinal foods. This is the first report of a novel, sensitive, and portable R-CDs based sensing platform for simple, cost-effective, and rapid on-site detection of CBZ in the field of food safety with great application potential.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Red-emissive carbon dot
Fluorescent nanosensor
Smartphone-assisted paper sensor
Carbendazim
Lotus seeds

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Quenching mechanism of CBZ on R-CDs
Optimization of sensing conditions for CBZ detection
Analytical performance of R-CDs based fluorescent nano sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FL excitation-emission matrix of R-CDs; (B) The UV–vis absorption spectra (Abs), and the excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) spectra of R-CDs.
Characterization regarding the (A) TEM, (B) HR-TEM images; (C) The distribution histogram of particle size, and (D) XRD pattern of R-CDs.

(A) The FL intensity of R-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of CBZ at pH 7; (B) FL intensity change of R-CDs based fluorescence nano sensor in the presence of CBZ (1 µg/mL) and other different interfering pesticides (10 µg/mL).

(A) Fabrication of fluorescent paper-based sensor, and smartphone-assisted analysis in ‘Color Grab’ app; (B) The red fluorescence of paper-based sensor gradually faded with the increase of CBZ concentration from 0 to 2 µg/mL.
Illustration of synthesis of R-CDs, and R-CDs based fluorescent and smartphone-assisted paper sensors for CBZ detection.
Comparison of the proposed fluorescent sensor with some previously sensing methods for CBZ analysis.
Recovery measured of CBZ in the spiked Lotus seed samples by the R-CDs based fluorescent nanosensor (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Red-emissive carbon dots based fluorescent and smartphone-integrated paper sensors for sensitive detection of carbendazim

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Traditional Chinese Medicine, Capital Medical University, Beijing 100069, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Trace detection of carbendazim (CBZ), a worldwide used pesticide, in crops and foods is important and urgent to reduce risks to human health. Herein, newly red-emissive carbon dots (R-CDs) based fluorescent and smartphone-integrated paper sensors were developed for highly-selective, and -sensitive rapid sensing of CBZ. R-CDs were synthesized from citric acid and urea via a DMF solvothermal method, which showed excellent analytical performance due to abundant carboxyl groups on the surface and long emission wavelength to avoid autofluorescence interference of matrix. According to the red fluorescence quenching by CBZ via π-π interaction and photoinduced electron-transfer effect, the newly fluorescent nano sensor could realize CBZ sensing within 3 min with high selectivity out of the other six interfering pesticides, as well as fascinating sensitivity with an ultralow detection limit of 3.5 ng/mL. The practicable application in the spiked medicinal Lotus seeds samples exhibited satisfactory recoveries of 95.5%-108.3%. The portable paper-based sensor fabricated by depositing R-CDs on the filter paper enabled quantitative readout of test strip results assisted with a smartphone, providing a rapid on-site tool for CBZ detection in medicinal foods. This is the first report of a novel, sensitive, and portable R-CDs based sensing platform for simple, cost-effective, and rapid on-site detection of CBZ in the field of food safety with great application potential.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbendazim (CBZ) is a systemic broad-spectrum benzimidazole antifungal that is commercially utilized worldwide for the management and avoidance of fungal infection on foods, vegetables, fruits, and medicinal herbs [1–3]. Nevertheless, the long-term and extensive use of CBZ has caused its residue in the related products and air, water, and soil [4]. Due to its poor water solubility, CBZ exhibits slow degradation and long residual time in diverse crops and the environment [5]. Recently, CBZ has become the focus of a heated discussion due to the growing concern about its cytotoxicity and embryotoxicity to cause endocrine disorders, infertility, and hepatic dysfunction [6,7]. In addition, CBZ is a known teratogen that can cause chromosomal abnormalities by damaging DNA. Epidemiological studies all over the world have confirmed the deleterious risks of CBZ pollution on animals and human health [8]. Although it has been classified as a dangerous chemical and placed on the priority list of endocrine in the European Commission with strict residual limits for a decade [9,10], CBZ still can be measured in various matrices. Therefore, the development of facile and reliable techniques for simple and on-site CBZ detection is of great significance and necessity.

Currently, an array of analytical strategies including chromatographic [11,12], surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [13,14], and electrochemistry [15,16] methods have been proposed for CBZ detection in diverse samples. Although displaying fascinating merits of high selectivity, sensitivity, and repeatability, these methods are limited by expensive and heavy instruments, complicated sample preparation, and high time consumption, making them powerless for rapid on-site analysis. As the ideal candidates, fluorescent sensors have attracted increasing interests for the determination of various targets in the analytical fields owing to their benefits of fast response, easy procedure, and short time consumption [17–19], providing a promising reference for CBZ detection.

In constructing a robust fluorescent sensor, the accurate selection of fluorescent materials is extremely important in scientific and industrial research to ensure real-time, rapid, economic, sensitive, non-destructive, and visible detection [20,21]. Of them, carbon dots (CDs) have obtained special investigative attention in many fields from sensing platforms to optoelectronic devices on account of their attractive merits, such as simple synthesis, tunable photoluminescence properties, small noxiousness, terrific water solubility and rich functional groups [17,22–24]. Most of these reported fluorescent sensors were established based on short-wavelength emission CDs with blue or green light. However, their optical applications were limited owing to the autofluorescence interference of matrix and photodamage in the UV excitation process. Then, red-emissive CDs (R-CDs) could effectively overcome these drawbacks thanks to their long emission wavelength greater than 600 nm, which have gained wide applications in bio-sensing, bio-imaging, and fingerprint identification, etc. [25–27]. However, there were no reports of the R-CDs based fluorescence sensing strategy for CBZ. In addition, smartphone-based paper sensing platform has appeared as advantageous advice considering measuring goal analytes in resource-constrained circumstance in view of the fact that they are low-cost, rapid, conveyable, simple operation, and environmentally friendly. The large specific area and good biocompatibility of the paper surface are conducive to the attachment of nanoparticles or the R-CDs composites. What’s more, smartphones with high-quality cameras and good data analysis capability to collect and analyze fluorescent image color values can miniaturize fluorescent sensors to make them more suitable for rapid on-site detection [28–30].
Detailed information on materials and apparatus was presented in the Supporting Information.

Red-emissive carbon dots (R-CDs) were synthesized based on the reported work [31] with appropriate changes. Shortly, 1 g citric acid and 2 g urea were dispersed in 10 mL DMF solvent and were sonicated for complete dissolution. The homogeneous solution was moved into a 25-mL Teflon-lined reactor and reacted at 180 °C for 4 h. After natural cooling at room temperature, the obtained solution was mixed with 20 mL ethanol for centrifugation at 8000 r/min for 10 min and the precipitation was collected. The above process was repeated to wash away residual solvents and small organic molecules. The washed precipitate was dissolved in 20 mL sodium hydroxide solution (50 mg/mL) with sonication for 10 min and then centrifuged at 16,000 r/min for 15 min. The precipitate was further dissolved in 20 mL hydrochloric solution (5 wt%) with sonication for 10 min and then collected the precipitate again with centrifugation at 16,000 r/min for 15 min. The obtained precipitation was re-dissolved in 15 mL water. After centrifuging at 16,000 r/min for 15 min to remove remained hydrochloric acid, the precipitate was collected and freeze-dried for the following experiments. After the purification, the product yield of the R-CDs was about 20%.

The fluorescent sensing procedure of the CBZ standard solution was performed as follows. The freeze-dried R-CDs powder was dissolved in ultrapure water to a final concentration of 0.1 mg/mL. Subsequently, the R-CDs solution was adjusted to pH 7.0 by adding NaOH solution. The fluorescence intensity of blank R-CDs was recorded as F 0. Then, 360 μL of R-CDs solution was incubated with 40 μL of various concentrations of CBZ standards. After reaction at room temperature for 3 min, the fluorescent intensity of R-CDs extinguished by CBZ was measured as F 1. All the fluorescent responses were recorded with excitation wavelength at 545 nm; All measurements were repeated three times.

To fabricate a fluorescent nano sensor with greatly selective for the accurate detection of CBZ, various interfering pesticides including acetamiprid, carbofuran, chlorpyrifos, metribuzin, imidacloprid, and malathion were examined for their influences on the red fluorescence emissive of R-CDs. Herewith, 360 μL of R-CDs aqueous solution was reacted with 40 μL of other pesticide solution (100 μg/mL). When the pesticide solution was incubated with the R-CDs at room temperature for 10 min, the fluorescence intensities of R-CDs impacted by various interfering pesticide molecules were recorded via fluorescence spectrophotometer. And the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs with CBZ (10 μg/mL) was also measured for comparison. The fluorescence quenching effects of CBZ and other interfering pesticides on the R-CDs were compared to assess the selectivity of the newly developed fluorescent nano sensor.

For confirming the practicality, accuracy, and reliability of the suggested fluorescent nano sensor for monitoring CBZ, Lotus seeds samples were chosen for the real sample test. Briefly, the confirmed CBZ-free Lotus seed sample was pulverized with a high-speed disintegrator and a 40-mesh sieve was used for obtaining fine powder. 1 g sample powder was added with different concentrations of CBZ standard solution and extracted with 5 mL of acetonitrile for 1 min on a vortex shaker, further to ultrasonic extracting for 10 min. Next, the resultant solution was centrifuged at 9000 r/min for 6 min at low temperature, and a 0.22-μm membrane was to filtrate the supernatant for removing larger particles. Eventually, the sample solution was weak tenfold by ultrapure water. The obtained solution was used for recovery evaluation to determine the amount of CBZ according to the above-mentioned procedure by using a fluorescence spectrometer.

The paper-based sensor is a portable analytical sensing platform with on-site detection capability. In this study, a smartphone-assisted paper-based sensor was fabricated on the test strip. First, the Whatman No. 1 filter paper was cut into a test strip in squares of 1 × 1 cm2. Each piece of paper-based test strip was immersed into 0.1 mg/mL of R-CDs solution as well as oven-dry at 40 °C for 1 h. Then, 0.2 mL of different concentrations (0–2 μg/mL) of CBZ standard solution was dropped onto the R-CDs coated test strip. After reaction for 10 min, the Redmi Note 11T pro+ digital phone camera was introduced to capture the fluorescent color changes of the test strips under 365 nm UV light. The camera parameters were set as white balance, and shutter time with ISO of 5500, 1/10, and 50, respectively, for clear fluorescence images. Regarding the data process, the test strip image was uploaded to the ‘Color Grab’ App to separate the RGB color space data. The ratio of the R channel to the B channel was used to quantify the amount of CBZ.

Citric acid and urea were selected as precursor molecules for the solvothermal synthesis of R-CDs in DMF. Compared with the hydrothermal method, DMF in the solvothermal processing could protect the conjugated sp2-domain of R-CDs from being destroyed, thereby forming large-sized conjugated sp2 domain, which provided the basis for the long wavelength fluorescence emissive of CDs [32]. The NaOH/HCl treatment to achieve deprotonation of functional groups on the R-CDs surface, which could increase the amount of carboxyl on the R-CDs surface (Scheme 1). This was evidenced by the significantly lower zeta potential (−42.3 mV) of R-CDs compared to green-emissive CDs (G-CDs) (−15.6 mV) untreated by NaOH/HCl (Figure S1). The n–π* transitions ascribe to the carboxyl on the R-CDs surface were the crucial source of red fluorescence, which has been confirmed by the latest work [33]. Therefore, the NaOH/HCl treatment not only increased the number of carboxyl groups on the R-CDs which benefited for CBZ recognition but also enabled R-CDs with the attractive red fluorescence emission.

The excitation-emission 3D fluorescence matrix image in Fig. 1 A showed that, after NaOH/HCl treatment, R-CDs displayed a single emission center with the maximal emissive wavelength at 620 nm under excited at 545 nm. And the maximum fluorescence intensity of the R-CDs was 33,956 a.u. under excitation at 545 nm. The fluorescence performance of R-CDs was further explored by measuring UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation also emission spectra. As exhibited in Fig. 1B, the UV–vis absorption curve (black) displayed the main band at about 550 nm, which probably was contributed by the surface defect states caused by the abundant carboxyl, overlapped basically with the fluorescence exciting curve of R-CDs (green). The fluorescence emissive curve (red) indicated the strongest fluorescence emissive wavelength of R-CDs was at around 620 nm. Then, it could be inferred that the UV absorption band at 550 nm caused by the surface defect state structure of R-CDs probably was the main reason for the graceful red fluorescence of R-CDs [34]. Figure S2 demonstrated the non-excitation dependence character of R-CDs under excited at 360–580 nm, which ascribe to the NaOH/HCl treatment for the modification of the surface defect state of R-CDs. In addition, the influence of different concentrations of sodium chloride solutions on the fluorescence of R-CDs was investigated. As shown in Figure S3, the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs was stable in the 0–100 mM NaCl solution, which indicated the potential of the R-CDs to construct the fluorescence sensors.

The structural feature of R-CDs was studied with TEM, HR-TEM, and X-ray diffraction (XRD) techniques. The TEM and HR-TEM pictures in Fig. 2 A and B showed that the synthesized R-CDs presented a homogeneous dispersal state in water with a clear-cut globular structure and the inset image showed the high crystallinity of R-CDs with an interplanar distance of 0.21 nm, consistent with the (1 0 0) face of graphene. Fig. 2C demonstrated that the sizes of R-CDs were mainly in the range from 0.9 to 3.9 nm with an average size of 2.24 nm. The XRD pattern of R-CDs in Fig. 2D exhibited a sharp peak at around 26°, which was for its (0 0 2) plane of graphite structure, signifying that the graphite construction was the main component in the core part of R-CDs [35].

The FT-IR and XPS spectrum analysis were introduced to analyze the surface functional groups, chemical bonds, and elemental composition of R-CDs. FT-IR spectrum was an essential mean to study the composition of surface functional groups on R-CDs. Figure S4A showed that the typical absorption peak of functional groups of R-CDs included O-H at 3448 cm−1, N-H at 3142 cm−1, and C = O at 1642 cm−1, indicating that the surface of R-CDs contained abundant carboxyl and amino groups, enable the good water-soluble of R-CDs. The peak at 2924 cm−1, 1480 cm−1, and 1277 cm−1 belonged to the typical stretching vibration of C-H, C = N, and C-N bonds, which could prove the formation of the conjugated structure in R-CDs. Figure S4B presented three usual binding energy peaks belonging to the C (61.73%), N (17.98%), and O (20.29%) elements at 284.78 eV, 399.68 eV, and 531.20 eV, respectively. The spectrum of C 1 s in Figure S4C displayed that three C-C/C = C (284.8 eV), C-N/C-O (286.7 eV), and O-C = O (288.1 eV) bonds mainly contained in R-CDs. The N 1 s spectrum in Figure S4D exhibited the pyrrolic N (399.7 eV) and graphitic-like/amino N (401.6 eV) groups were the main components of N elements in R-CDs. And the O1s spectrum in Figure S4E could be resolved into two parts the C-O (531.1 eV) and C = O (532.4 eV) bonds, respectively [36].

Through comprehensive analysis of these characterization data of R-CDs, it could be inferred that R-CDs were spherical nanodots with a conjugated skeleton composed of C, N as the core, and the surface of which was covered by abundant carboxyl, amino, and other groups. The ample carboxyl groups of R-CDs not only endowed R-CDs with good water solubility but as well enable R-CDs with red emissive, which laid the foundation for the sensitive sensing of CBZ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching mechanism of CBZ on R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Usually, the fluorescence quenching mechanism mainly includes the dynamic quenching effect (DQE), static quenching effect (SQE), Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), inner filter effect (IFE), and photoinduced electron-transfer (PET) [37]. DQE can be understood as the crash of the quencher with the excited CDs, and the collision process is usually accompanied by electron and energy transfer. Therefore, the lifetime of CDs will be changed by the quencher in DQE. SQE is usually accompanied by the formation of new non-fluorescent complexes on the CDs surface by the quencher. Thus, the complex usually changes the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs, but the fluorescence lifetime of CDs will not change. The occurrence of FRET and IFE requires the overlap of the fluorescence emission or excitation spectrum of CDs with the absorption spectrum of the quencher. PET can be simply explained as electron transfer between CDs and the quencher, and the fluorescence lifetime of R-CDs will be reduced by the quencher [38].

Herein, aiming to explore the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CBZ on R-CDs, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CBZ and fluorescence spectra of R-CDs were recorded. Figure S5 revealed that the UV–vis absorption peak of CBZ was mainly located at 200–300 nm, which exhibited no intersection with the fluorescence spectra of R-CDs (545 nm exciting and 620 nm emissive), thus, FRET and IFE would not occur. Then, the changes in fluorescence lifetime and UV–vis spectrum of R-CDs caused by CBZ were studied. Figure S6A proved the addition of CBZ did not significantly shift the absorption curve of R-CDs, signifying that no nonfluorescent complex was generated during the quenching process, which would not lead to SQE. For the further revelation of the quenching mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime decay curves of R-CDs and the R-CDs + CBZ system were tested. Figure S6B showed that the average fluorescence lifetime of R-CDs was 1.98 ns, and that of the R-CDs + CBZ system was 1.66 ns. CBZ reduced the fluorescence decay lifetime of R-CDs from 1.98 ns to 1.66 ns, therefore, the quenching of CBZ on R-CDs could be first determined as a dynamic quenching process.

It could be inferred that during the dynamic quenching process, the large-sized conjugated sp2 domain of R-CDs could easily recognize CBZ through π-π stacking. Subsequently, the interaction between the amino groups of CBZ and the abundant carboxyl on the R-CDs surface resulted in electron transfer between R-CDs and CBZ, which disrupted n–π* transitions on the R-CDs surface. The electron transfer was based on the PET effect [39] because the abundant carboxyl groups could function as favorable electron donors to facilitate the PET effect, and CBZ could accept and transfer electrons from R-CDs to make R-CDs return to the ground state. In short, the quenching of CBZ on R-CDs was a dynamic process due to the PET effect between the carboxyl on the R-CDs surface and the amino of CBZ. These suggested that the quenching mechanism of R-CDs by CBZ was primarily based on PET.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of sensing conditions for CBZ detection
ce:section.ce:para: Aiming to realize the optimal sensing performance of the R-CDs based fluorescent nano sensors for CBZ detection, several vital test conditions, such as the added concentration of R-CDs, solution pH, type of buffer, and reacting time, were optimized.

When the added concentration of R-CDs was too high, the π-π absorption and interaction between CDs themselves would occur aggregation-caused quenching [40]. To ensure that the sensing system contained a sufficient number of R-CDs for CBZ detection without aggregation-induced quenching, the concentration of R-CDs was optimized to a suitable concentration. 20 mg/mL of R-CDs initial water solution was prepared to different concentrations of R-CDs solution, and the fluorescence intensities of different concentrations of R-CDs solutions at 620 nm were compared. Figure S7A indicated that the largest FL intensity was obtained when the concentration of R-CDs solution was 0.1 mg/mL. Thus, 0.1 mg/mL of R-CDs solution was used in the subsequent experiments.

The change in sensing system pH could affect the protonation/deprotonation process on the R-CDs surface, thereby changing the electron distribution and fluorescence intensity. Therefore, the changes in fluorescence responses of the sensing system affected by CBZ at pH 4–9 were explored. Figure S7B showed that the changes of fluorescence intensity (ΔF = F 0 - F 1) caused by CBZ increased gradually with increasing pH from 4 to 7, and then were stabilized or slightly lowered when solution pH was larger than 7. This illustrated that strong acidic conditions caused small fluorescence quenching, while neutral or alkaline conditions presented a high impact on the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs. It might be owing to the deprotonation process of functional groups on the R-CDs surface in a neutral or alkaline environment, which enhanced the n → π* interaction between the carboxyl on R-CDs and the electron donor groups in solvent, thereby improving the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs. The enhanced fluorescence intensity of R-CDs (F 0) caused the increase of ΔF with the addition of CBZ. Therefore, the solution system pH was controlled at 7.

The buffer solution could maintain the sensing system pH within a certain range for protecting the impact of the changed pH on the sensing system. Different conjugate acid-base pairs contained in diverse buffer solutions would exhibit various effects on the fluorescent sensing performance. Herein, five types of buffer systems including 0.01 M Tris-HCl, 1 × HEPES, 0.01 M PBS, and 0.01 M PBST at pH 7 and ultrapure water were investigated. As Figure S7C displayed that the highest fluorescence intensity change (ΔF) was obtained when ultrapure water was selected as the solvent. This indicated different conjugated acid-base pairs and substances contained in the former four types of buffer solution, which would affect the recognition ability of R-CDs for CBZ, as well as the PET process between them. Therefore, ultrapure water was chosen as the sensing system solvent.

The reacting time affected the recognition ability of R-CDs to CBZ. Thus, the ΔF value of the R-CDs + CBZ solution at a diverse incubation time of 0–21 min was investigated. Figure S7D indicated that the ΔF value arrived steady after a 3-min reacting time of R-CDs with CBZ. Therefore, CBZ could completely quench R-CDs fluorescence within 3 min as well as the newly R-CDs based fluorescence nano sensor could achieve rapid detection of CBZ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of R-CDs based fluorescent nano sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical performance of the R-CDs based fluorescence nano sensor following the optimal conditions was assessed by measuring the fluorescence spectra of R-CDs (0.1 mg/mL) with different concentrations of CBZ (0, 10, 50, 100, 140, 180, 200, 250, 300, 400 ng/mL). Fig. 3 A displayed that the fluorescence emissive intensity at 620 nm reduced with increasing the added concentration of CBZ under 545 nm excitation. A good linear correlation between ΔF and concentration of CBZ within the range of 10–250 ng/mL was obtained with a linear expression ΔF = 20.505x + 36.4967 (R 2 = 0.996). The limit of detection (LOD) with 3.5 ng/mL was obtained which was calculated by the formula LOD = (3σ/k). Therefore, the new sensing platform was capable of detecting CBZ at an extremely low level in 3 min and quantifying it in this range. Compared with other reported sensor methods for CBZ determination in Table 1 , this novel R-CDs based fluorescent nanosensor presented excellent LOD and sensing performance, which would realize sensitive, accurate, low-cost, and rapid quantification of CBZ within 3 min.

The selectivity of this newly R-CDs based fluorescent nano sensor was examined by comparing the ΔF value of R-CDs after reacting with other six pesticides, such as acetamiprid, carbofuran, chlorpyrifos, metribuzin, imidacloprid, and malathion. As shown in Fig. 3B, under the same experimental conditions, 10 μg/mL of the other six pesticides hardly caused changes (ΔF) in the fluorescence intensity of the system, while 1 μg/mL of CBZ, that was 1/10 of the concentration of the interfering pesticides, could cause a significant fluorescence intensity change. These indicated that the synthesized R-CDs could specifically recognize CBZ and trigger fluorescence quenching, which might be due to the role of amino groups of CBZ molecules. The findings revealed that the R-CDs based fluorescent nano sensor could selectively respond to CBZ with high specificity over the other six pesticides, implying great application potential in real samples.

CBZ was usually found with residue in a variety of foods including medicinal foods. Therefore, in this work, Lotus seeds with high edible functions and medicinal values were chosen as the real samples to evaluate the practicality and stability of this newly R-CDs based fluorescent nano sensor for CBZ detection through spiking experiments. These confirmed CBZ-free Lotus seed samples were crushed and sieved into a fine powder and three concentrations levels (50, 100, and 200 ng/mL) of CBZ standards were introduced, further the above-mentioned procedure for sample solution preparation and recovery tests. Table 2 submitted the satisfying recoveries from 95.5% to 108.3% with relative standard deviations (RSDs) lower than 7.0%, confirming the great practicality and stability of the R-CDs based fluorescent nano sensor as well as the remarkable anti-interference capability to other pesticides and complex matrix components.

To improve the rapid and portable on-site detection capability of this newly developed fluorescent sensing platform, a simple and convenient fluorescent paper-based sensor was further fabricated for the visual measurement of CBZ by depositing R-CDs directly on the filter paper. Fig. 4 A exhibited that the test paper showed a highly uniform red luminescence by a 365-nm UV lamp exciting. It indicated that the deposition of R-CDs in the cellulose paper did not cause self-quenching and could be used for further smartphone-assisted paper-based detection. As seen from Fig. 4B, the red fluorescence of the test paper excited by a 365 nm UV lamp gradually faded and the red color of the images of the paper strips became darker with the concentration of CBZ increasing from 0 to 2 μg/mL, indicating that the increased amount of CBZ caused more fluorescence reduction of R-CDs. These images were photographed by using a smartphone and the built-in ‘Color Grab’ App was introduced to analyze the taken images of the paper strips (Fig. 4A). A favorable linear relationship was observed between the R/B ratio of the red channel to the blue channel and CBZ concentration, exhibiting a linear equation of y = -0.5547x + 1.9861 (R 2 = 0.983) within of 0–2 μg/mL range. This suggested that R-CDs deposition paper-based sensor assisted with a smartphone was reliable and feasible for rapid on-site detection of CBZ. Future work would focus on improving the reusability of this R-CDs based paper sensor by introducing more durable papers for its wide real-world applications.

In this work, the novel R-CDs based facile fluorescent and smartphone-integrated paper sensors were developed for highly selective and rapid on-spot sensing of trace CBZ in medicinal Lotus seeds. R-CDs containing abundant carboxyl on the surface were synthesized with citric acid and urea as precursor molecules in DMF by solvothermal method. Various characterization results have confirmed the good water solubility and excellent fluorescence properties of R-CDs without autofluorescence interference of the Lotus seed matrix. The abundant carboxyl on R-CDs surface could recognize CBZ to cause fluorescence quenching through the PET effect.

Based on this fluorescence quenching phenomenon, the newly fabricated fluorescent nano sensor could realize highly-selective and sensitive sensing of CBZ within 3 min. Under the optimized conditions, this R-CDs based fluorescent nano sensor allowed an ultralow LOD of 3.5 ng/mL for CBZ in a wide concentration range of 10–250 ng/mL. The great reliability of the developed fluorescent nano sensor for actual complicated samples was confirmed by the practical application in medicinal Lotus seed samples with satisfactory recoveries of 95.5–108.3%. Moreover, a smartphone-assisted paper sensor was fabricated and demonstrated with excellent quantitative detection capability toward CBZ. Most interestingly, the RGB color values of the paper strip could be identified by the smartphone’s built-in ‘Color Grab’ App, proving a simple, instrument-free, rapid on-site visual analysis strategy for pesticide residues.
In summary, this study provides a new strategy for simple, cost-effective, and on-site rapid detection of CBZ residue in foods and other samples with great application potential.

Haipeng Guo: Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Ruiqi Yang: Resources, Methodology. Doudou Lei: Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Xiao Ma: Formal analysis, Resources. Dingkun Zhang: Validation, Investigation. Peng Li: Formal analysis. Jiabo Wang: Conceptualization, Supervision. Lidong Zhou: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Weijun Kong: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (81973474, 82274089), the Open Research Fund of State Key Laboratory of Southwestern Chinese Medicine Resources (SKLTCM2022022), and Beijing Natural Science Foundation (7232265).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.108586.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
